Colosseum: Road to Freedom – The Brutal Beauty of Becoming a Legend
A PS2 review of Colosseum: Road to Freedom, Koei’s gritty gladiator RPG where debt, blood, training and survival collide in the sand.
A rough cult classic with real bite
A clunky but deeply compelling gladiator RPG that rewards patience, planning and a taste for brutal PS2-era ambition.
Players who enjoy cult PS2 games, gladiator stories, RPG progression and tactical arena combat.
The blend of training, debt, combat styles and high-risk arena spectacle.
Stiff movement, rough presentation and clunky pacing between fights.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The eternal struggle in the sand

There is something instantly gripping about the idea of fighting for your freedom.

Not saving the world.

Not collecting magic crystals.

Not stopping an ancient evil from returning.

Just surviving long enough to earn your life back.

That is the brutal appeal of *Colosseum: Road to Freedom*. Released on PlayStation 2, this lesser-known Koei title throws you into the blood, dust and danger of ancient Rome, then asks one simple question:

Can you become a legend before the arena breaks you?

It is not a smooth game. It is not an easy game. It is not even a particularly welcoming game.

But it has something many cleaner, shinier games lack.

Identity.

*Colosseum: Road to Freedom* is strange, ambitious, awkward and deeply committed to its fantasy. It wants you to feel like a gladiator, not a superhero wearing sandals. Every fight hurts. Every mistake matters. Every coin earned feels like it was dragged out of the sand with broken fingers.

And that is exactly why it still stands out.

## Your life, your glory, your debt

The setup is wonderfully direct.

You are a gladiator in debt.

A huge amount of debt.

Your goal is to pay it off within a limited number of days by fighting, training and clawing your way up through the arena ranks. It is a simple structure, but it gives the entire game a constant pressure that works surprisingly well.

You are not just fighting because the game tells you to.

You are fighting because time is running out.

Every day matters. Every event matters. Every training session matters. That gives *Colosseum* a satisfying routine. Train, prepare, fight, earn, upgrade, repeat. On paper, that sounds repetitive. In practice, the pressure of the debt system gives it purpose.

The role-playing elements help too.

At the start, you create your gladiator, choosing their background, origin, path and deity. These choices shape your starting stats and give the game a stronger RPG flavour than you might expect from a gladiator action game.

This is not just about swinging a sword.

It is about building a fighter.

Strength, dexterity, agility, vitality and stamina all matter. So do weapon styles. You can lean into sword and shield, two-handed weapons or unarmed fighting, each changing the way you approach combat.

It gives the game a rough but satisfying sense of ownership.

Your gladiator is not handed to you fully formed.

You make them.

Then the arena tests whether you built them well enough.

## Training before glory

Between fights, training becomes its own little ritual.

You perform drills, build stats and prepare for the next day’s violence. Sit-ups, dodging, mock duels and other exercises feed into your progression, giving you skill points and helping shape your fighter’s strengths.

It is hardly glamorous.

But that is part of the charm.

The training system reinforces the idea that glory is earned through repetition. You do not just stroll into the Colosseum and become a legend. You grind. You prepare. You improve inch by inch.

This also gives the game a strange sports-career quality.

You are managing a fighter’s body, schedule, equipment and survival. You are thinking about tomorrow while still recovering from yesterday. The fantasy is less “heroic warrior” and more “desperate athlete with a sword and a debt collector breathing down his neck.”

That distinction matters.

It makes every victory feel more personal.

## Combat: heavy, awkward and strangely satisfying

The combat is where *Colosseum: Road to Freedom* both shines and struggles.

This is not fast, fluid action by modern standards. Your gladiator moves with weight. Attacks can feel stiff. Swings miss. Camera angles can betray you. Enemy behaviour can move from foolish to terrifying in seconds.

At first, it can feel clumsy.

Then you start to understand it.

The combat is not about elegance. It is about commitment. Positioning matters. Timing matters. Weapon choice matters. Awareness matters. If you charge in carelessly, you will get punished. If you let yourself get surrounded, you will probably end up face-down in the dirt.

When it clicks, there is a nasty satisfaction to it.

Landing a clean strike, disarming an opponent, surviving a brutal exchange or turning a bad situation into a crowd-pleasing comeback feels fantastic. Not because the mechanics are silky smooth, but because they feel hard-earned.

This is combat with grit under its fingernails.

It is messy, but it has tension.

## The arena as theatre

The fights are not limited to simple one-on-one duels.

You get team battles, survival matches, hunts, duels and larger historical spectacles. Some matches throw animals into the mix. Others recreate famous battles in exaggerated, game-like form. You might be fighting men one day, bulls the next and something completely absurd after that.

This variety keeps the game alive.

The best events feel like ancient Roman theatre pushed through a PS2 filter. Chariots, towers, animals, chaos and instant-death hazards all create moments where the game suddenly feels much bigger than its rough presentation suggests.

That is one of *Colosseum’s* greatest tricks.

It is technically limited, but conceptually bold.

The arenas may look plain by modern standards, yet the situations they create are memorable. You are not just fighting enemies. You are surviving events.

And survival feels good.

## RPG depth in the dust

What makes *Colosseum: Road to Freedom* more interesting than a simple arena brawler is the amount of customisation underneath.

The skill system lets you shape your gladiator with passive buffs and active abilities. You can build a durable tank, a nimble duelist, a weapon-focused bruiser or a scrappy fighter who survives through movement and stamina.

It is not the most elegant RPG system ever made, but it gives the game a strong sense of progression.

You are always chasing something.

Better stats.
Better skills.
Better equipment.
Better rank.
Better pay.

That constant hunger is what keeps the loop moving.

There is also a satisfying risk-and-reward element to how you collect new skills and tablets during fights. Playing boldly can pay off, but greed can get you killed. That fits the gladiator fantasy perfectly.

The game wants you to take chances.

It also wants you to remember that arrogance gets people buried.

## Rough around the edges

Let’s be honest.

*Colosseum: Road to Freedom* is not a polished masterpiece.

The visuals are functional rather than beautiful. Animations are stiff. Environments repeat. Sound effects can grate. The menus are awkward, and the overall presentation has that mid-budget PS2 roughness that can either charm you or drive you mad.

The pacing can also drag.

Moving around hubs feels slower than it should. Some parts between matches feel stretched out. Equipment management is not as smooth as it needs to be, especially in a game where gear matters so much.

There is a great game here, but you sometimes have to meet it halfway.

That will be the breaking point for some players.

If you need modern smoothness, *Colosseum* will feel ancient in the wrong way.

But if you can accept the stiffness, there is something really compelling underneath.

## Why it still matters

The reason *Colosseum: Road to Freedom* is worth revisiting is not because it is flawless.

It is worth revisiting because it is specific.

It has a vision.

It wants to be a gladiator life simulator as much as an action game. It wants you to feel trapped by debt, shaped by training and tested by combat. It wants every victory to feel like a step toward freedom rather than just another completed level.

That sense of commitment gives it lasting value.

Many PS2 games have aged more gracefully in terms of controls and presentation. Fewer have aged with this much personality intact.

There is a seriousness to *Colosseum* that makes its sillier moments easier to forgive. It understands the drama of the arena. It understands the fantasy of rising from nothing. It understands that becoming a legend should hurt.

And in its best moments, it delivers exactly that.

## Final verdict

*Colosseum: Road to Freedom* is a cult classic in the truest sense.

It is too clunky to recommend without warning, but too interesting to dismiss. Its combat can frustrate, its presentation can creak, and its pacing can test your patience.

But beneath all of that is a gritty, ambitious gladiator RPG with real depth and a powerful sense of identity.

It is *Gladiator* through a PS2 lens.

Part action game.
Part RPG.
Part sports career mode.
Part punishment simulator.

For the right player, that combination is gold.

Not polished gold.

More like a battered old coin pulled from the sand.

But gold all the same.

Win enough fights, train hard enough and survive long enough, and *Colosseum: Road to Freedom* becomes something special.

A flawed game about earning freedom.

And somehow, that makes its flaws feel fitting.

Def Jam: Fight for NY – Blazin’ Beats & Broken Teeth
A PS2 review of Def Jam: Fight for NY, EA’s chaotic hip-hop brawler that turns street fighting, celebrity cameos and brutal arena combat into one of the most entertaining fighters of its era.
Brutal, stylish and still ridiculously fun
One of the PS2’s most chaotic and memorable fighters, packed with style, personality and bone-crunching energy.
Players who love arcade brawlers, hip-hop culture and over-the-top PS2-era chaos.
A brilliant blend of wrestling, street fighting, music, arenas and celebrity spectacle.
Knockdowns and wall collisions can occasionally slow the rhythm of a fight.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Drop the mic, then drop your enemies

Def Jam: Fight for NY is one of those games that sounds like it should not work.

A celebrity-filled hip-hop fighting game where Snoop Dogg runs the underground, Method Man can smash people through scenery, and your custom fighter can become a blinged-out menace with a wardrobe budget bigger than most RPG towns.

On paper, it sounds ridiculous.

In motion, it is ridiculous.

But it is also brilliant.

Released on PlayStation 2 in 2004, Def Jam: Fight for NY takes the wrestling foundations of Def Jam Vendetta and mutates them into something louder, rougher and far more memorable. This is not just a licensed fighting game with famous faces thrown in for attention. It is a full-blown street brawler with real mechanical depth, absurd energy and enough personality to fill an entire record shop.

It is violent, stylish, silly and incredibly playable.

Exactly the kind of PS2 game that makes you miss the era when big publishers were willing to make strange things with confidence.

## The fighting words

The real magic of Fight for NY is the combat system.

This is not a simple button-mashing brawler where celebrity novelty does all the heavy lifting. Under the swagger and soundtrack is a surprisingly strong fighting engine built around different styles, momentum, positioning and brutal environmental damage.

Your fighter can learn from several disciplines, including wrestling, kickboxing, martial arts, street fighting and submissions. Each style changes the way you approach a fight. Wrestling gives you throws and grapples. Kickboxing gives you striking range. Submissions let you target limbs and drain opponents down in a nastier, more methodical way.

The beauty is in the mix.

You are not simply choosing a moveset. You are building a fighting identity.

The game lets you create a brawler who feels like a nightclub tank, a martial arts showboat, a dirty street scrapper or some deeply unpleasant blend of all three. It gives every fight a bit of personal flavour, especially once your created character starts earning money, clothes, jewellery and respect.

Then there are the Blazin’ Moves.

These finishers are pure PS2 theatre. They are loud, excessive and painful-looking in a way only this generation could deliver. A full momentum bar turns the match into a stage, the camera leans in, and someone is about to have a very bad evening.

It never gets old.

## The arena is part of the fight

One of the smartest things Fight for NY does is make its arenas feel active.

The environments are not just backdrops. They are weapons.

You can slam opponents into walls, throw them into crowds, smash them through objects and use the edges of each arena to turn a close fight into a disaster. The crowd itself adds to the madness, grabbing fighters, handing over weapons and turning every scrap into something that feels like a street-level spectacle.

This gives matches a messy, physical rhythm.

You are not just watching health bars. You are watching space. You are looking for openings near walls, objects and crowd sections. You are trying to keep control while the arena itself threatens to join in.

That is where Fight for NY separates itself from more traditional fighters.

It does not want to be clean.

It wants to be chaotic.

And it understands exactly why that is fun.

## From the underground up

The story mode is far better than it has any right to be.

You play as a custom fighter who saves D-Mob and gets pulled into the violent world of New York’s underground fight scene. From there, you climb the ranks, build your reputation, earn cash and scrap your way through a roster full of rappers, fighters, criminals and original characters.

It is pulp nonsense, but it is entertaining pulp nonsense.

Snoop Dogg plays Crow, the main villain, and the whole thing has the energy of a straight-to-DVD crime film that somehow got given a huge roster and a brilliant combat system. The voice acting is not exactly subtle, but that is part of the charm. Everyone sounds like they are having fun, or at least like they know exactly what kind of game this is.

The progression loop is genuinely satisfying too.

Win fights. Earn money. Buy clothes. Train new styles. Improve your stats. Look more ridiculous. Hit harder.

It is wonderfully gamey in the best way.

And yes, in true Def Jam fashion, style matters. Looking good is not just cosmetic dressing. It feeds into your charisma and momentum, which means the drip is part of the build.

That is absurd.

That is also completely perfect.

## Strength in numbers

The roster is stacked.

Def Jam: Fight for NY is packed with recognisable names, from Method Man and Redman to Ludacris, Busta Rhymes, Fat Joe, Sean Paul, Ice-T and Snoop Dogg. It also includes actors and original fighters who help round out the world.

What makes the roster work is that these characters are not just skins.

They feel like exaggerated fighting-game versions of themselves. Everyone is pushed through the same grimy, comic-book filter. The result is a cast that feels ridiculous, but consistent within the world the game creates.

It is celebrity fan service, absolutely.

But it is celebrity fan service with mechanics behind it.

That is the difference.

Instead of feeling like a cheap gimmick, the roster becomes part of the game’s identity. You are not just picking a famous face. You are picking a fighter, a style, a personality and a finishing move that will probably make you wince.

## The sound of broken teeth

A game like this lives and dies by attitude, and Fight for NY has plenty of it.

The soundtrack is aggressive, era-specific and perfectly matched to the grime of the arenas. It gives each match a punchy, club-like energy. The sound effects do their part too. Every slam, strike and collision lands with a satisfying thud.

This is not elegant combat.

This is bodies hitting concrete, boots scraping floors and crowds screaming for someone to get finished.

The presentation is pure early-2000s PS2 excess. Menus are loud. Character customisation is flashy. The whole thing feels like it was built to be played on a chunky television with the volume slightly too high.

And honestly, that is part of the appeal.

## The comedown

For all its strengths, Fight for NY is not flawless.

The biggest frustration is the rhythm-breaking knockdown system. When you get slammed into a wall, battered by the crowd or caught in a horrible chain of punishment, the recovery can feel painfully slow. Sometimes you are waiting for your fighter to get up while your opponent is already preparing to ruin your life again.

It is not enough to spoil the game, but it can make certain fights feel clunkier than they should.

The voice acting is also objectively ridiculous. Some performances are funny. Some are wooden. Some sound like someone recorded them while already halfway out the door.

But in a strange way, that adds to the charm.

Fight for NY is not polished in the modern sense.

It is loud, scrappy, confident and slightly unhinged.

That is the whole point.

## Why it still works

Def Jam: Fight for NY still works because it understands identity.

It is not trying to be Tekken. It is not trying to be Street Fighter. It is not even really trying to be a wrestling game anymore.

It is its own thing.

A street brawler with fighting-game depth.
A licensed celebrity showcase with real mechanical substance.
A PS2-era experiment that somehow aged into a cult classic.

Modern games are often cleaner, smoother and safer. Fight for NY is none of those things. It is rough around the edges, but full of life. Every match feels like something is about to go wrong, and that unpredictability is exactly why it remains so entertaining.

It is a reminder of a time when games could be big, weird and wildly specific.

Nobody was asking for a hip-hop wrestling street fighter with RPG-lite progression and brutal environmental combat.

But we got one.

And it slapped.

## Final verdict

Def Jam: Fight for NY is one of the PlayStation 2’s great licensed oddities.

It takes a premise that could have been disposable and turns it into one of the most memorable brawlers of its generation. The combat has depth, the arenas have personality, the roster is absurdly fun, and the whole thing carries itself with a swagger that few games can match.

It is messy in places.

It is dated in others.

But it is also creative, confident and endlessly entertaining.

Whether you are here for the fighting, the music, the customisation or simply the joy of throwing a famous rapper through a wall, Fight for NY still delivers.

Just try not to get stuck in the corner.

Silent Hill 2: In my restless dreams, I see that town
A retrospective on Silent Hill 2, Team Silent’s psychological horror masterpiece, and why its fog, guilt, monsters and memory still linger decades later.

---------

Some games are played. Others are endured.

But Silent Hill 2?

It is experienced.

It does not just unsettle. It lingers. Not like a jump scare, but like a whispered secret you cannot forget. Released in 2001 on the PlayStation 2, Konami’s psychological horror epic rewrote the rules for the genre. It defied expectations, tore up horror clichés, and offered a journey not through a haunted town, but through a haunted mind.

In this retrospective, we revisit Silent Hill 2 through the smudged, CRT-glow of the PS2 version. This is not just a trip back to one of the finest survival horror games ever made. It is a deep dive into a game that dared to make guilt the true villain, then dared us to face our worst selves.

## Introducing the nightmare: James Sunderland’s fatal pilgrimage

“Mary died of that damn disease three years ago… So then why did I get a letter from her?”

With those words, James Sunderland begins his descent into hell. Not the fire-and-brimstone kind, but one forged from grief, regret and psychological trauma.

Unlike most horror heroes, James is not here to rescue anyone. He is not escaping a viral outbreak. He is responding to a letter from his dead wife.

It is a genius hook.

Silent Hill, the town, becomes more than a setting. It becomes a mirror. A mutable, shifting hellscape shaped not simply by occult forces, but by the protagonist’s psyche. Every alley, every hallway, every locked door reflects something back at James and, by extension, the player.

It is subtle.

Sinister.

And utterly singular in gaming.

## The PS2 edition: hardware limitation alchemy

Released at the height of the PlayStation 2’s dominance, Silent Hill 2 pushed the console’s hardware in uncanny directions.

Where other titles tried to wow players with glitzy explosions or sprawling cities, Silent Hill 2 used its limitations as a weapon.

The now-iconic fog, originally employed to hide draw distance, becomes a key component of the atmosphere. It cloaks horrors. It disorients. It isolates.

Indoors, darkness becomes the game’s co-conspirator, forcing the player to rely on a flickering torchlight that casts haunting shadows around James like spectres of his conscience.

Textures are grimy, smeared and sickly, but intentionally so. The environments are not meant to impress. They are meant to disturb.

Water in the Lakeview Hotel looks like a memory melting. Blood does not drip. It stains, sears and lingers.

And through it all, the PS2 keeps up. Frame rates are steady, load times are brief, and transitions feel seamless. The town may be cursed, but the engine is blessed.

## Character study: human horror on full display

Where most horror games populate their worlds with monsters, Silent Hill 2 populates its story with people.

That is far more terrifying.

Each character James meets is a fractured psyche walking the same foggy paths, but carrying different burdens.

Angela Orosco is perhaps the most harrowing. Abused by her father and trapped in despair, Angela’s journey through the town is one of increasing hopelessness. Her world is quite literally on fire, reflecting her inner torment. Her story does not end with victory, but resignation.

And it is haunting.

Eddie Dombrowski, a nervous man with a strained laugh, seems like comic relief at first. Then the bodies start to stack up. Eddie is a portrait of violence enabled by delusion. As his mind frays, so does yours, because you realise what Silent Hill has done to him and what it might do to James.

Laura, the orphaned child, offers rare innocence, but even she is twisted by perspective. She views Mary and James through a different lens, offering jarring truths. Her presence complicates James’ version of events.

Then there is Maria.

Perhaps the greatest enigma in gaming.

She is Mary, but she is not. She is fantasy, memory, guilt and longing, all sewn together into something impossible. Her flirtations feel wrong. Her repeated deaths feel worse. Is she bait? Punishment? Hope?

She is all of them.

And nothing.

## Pyramid Head: the monster who is not a monster

Let’s be clear. Pyramid Head is not just a cool enemy.

He is a concept.

A psychological manifestation.

A being born from James’ subconscious desire for punishment.

He does not talk. He does not chase in the usual horror-game sense. He stalks. His movements are ponderous, deliberate and awful. Each appearance feels like another blow to James and to the player.

His design is angular, rusted, industrial and immediately iconic. He is not simply a boss. He is an accusation.

When you finally confront him, and he kneels defeated only for the horror to double, it does not feel like victory. It feels like a reckoning.

His later appearances in sequels, films and crossovers have diluted his symbolism. But here, in Silent Hill 2, he is perfect.

A monster born from moral rot.

## Monsters as metaphor: the bestiary of broken minds

Silent Hill 2’s creature design is grotesquely poetic.

These are not random beasts. They are ideas made flesh.

Lying Figures are humanoid, wrapped in straitjacket-like flesh. They spray acid, perhaps bile, perhaps venom, representing James’ suppression and internalised guilt.

Mannequins are leg-for-torso monstrosities, motionless until provoked. Their overt sexuality and violent movement speak to repression and desire.

Bubble Head Nurses are twisted icons of care turned threat. Fetishised, faceless and dangerous, they become manifestations of Mary’s illness and James’ conflicted lust.

Abstract Daddy, a contorted bedlike creature, appears during Angela’s scenes and is horrifyingly symbolic of her trauma.

No enemy is just an enemy.

Each is tied to suffering.

This is not creature design. This is psychoanalysis with teeth.

## Gameplay loop: slow, methodical and maddeningly tense

Silent Hill 2 does not give you jump scares every five minutes.

It gives you dread.

Lingering, festering dread.

Combat is functional at best. James can swing pipes, swing boards or fire pistols with aim so shaky you would swear he was half-cut. But that is the point. He is not a soldier. He is broken.

The game does not want you to win fights. It wants you to fear them.

Exploration is masterful. Keys are hidden in disturbing places. Puzzles range from literary riddles about sin to strange item combinations that feel like they belong in a nightmare.

And the map system, scratched with handwritten notes, locked doors and red marks, keeps you grounded in a town designed to disorient.

## Symbolism in everything: nothing is incidental

It cannot be overstated. Silent Hill 2 is a game that means.

Everything in it, every visual, every line, every ambient sound, carries symbolic weight.

The fog is not just atmospheric masking. It represents James’ self-imposed blindness.

The town morphs depending on the psychological state of each character. For James, it is a purgatory of guilt. For Angela, it is a hell of fire and abuse.

The mirrors found throughout the game reflect more than James’ image. They invite players to reflect on themselves.

Maria’s constant deaths are not cheap shock tactics. They are James’ guilt replaying his wife’s final moments over and over in slightly altered forms.

Rooms loop endlessly. Doors slam shut behind you. Architecture shifts without warning.

Everything plays into the dream logic of trauma and denial.

Then there is the final act: the Lakeview Hotel. This location is not just the climax of the plot. It is the truth laid bare. The waterlogged walls, dilapidated decor and haunted videotape are all steeped in narrative catharsis.

## Soundtrack and sound design: Akira Yamaoka’s masterwork

If there is a Mount Rushmore of video game composers, Akira Yamaoka deserves a spot for Silent Hill 2 alone.

His soundtrack is a symphony of sorrow. From the melancholy chords of Theme of Laura to the ambient horror of the hospital basement, the music is the mood.

Equally impressive is the sound design. Floorboards creak. Radios hum. Wind howls. Distant groans reverberate through rusted halls. Each step asks a question:

“What did I just hear?”

The answer is rarely kind.

The voice acting is offbeat and stilted, but here is the strange thing: it works. The delivery feels dreamlike and otherworldly. It adds to the atmosphere rather than detracting from it.

## Technical craftsmanship on the PS2

On paper, Silent Hill 2 does not push polygons like Metal Gear Solid 2 or animate crowds like Final Fantasy X.

But in terms of atmosphere, it is peerless.

The PlayStation 2 version achieves its emotional power through subtlety and manipulation.

Dynamic lighting plays a critical role. The player’s torchlight casts real-time shadows that jitter and stretch in disorienting ways, heightening tension. The grain filter gives the visuals a filmic quality that has aged far better than the clean, plasticky look of many early 2000s games.

On the audio front, the PS2’s sound chip is pushed to eerie limits. Stereo panning creates the illusion of creatures lurking just out of sight. The feedback from your radio, which crackles louder as enemies approach, becomes a Pavlovian trigger for panic.

Even the UI is minimalist. Your health is indicated by colour shifts rather than numbers, forcing you to engage intuitively, not analytically.

This technical minimalism is not a limitation.

It is design brilliance.

## The end: who are you, James?

Few games have endings this reactive.

Depending on how you play, not what you choose, but what you do, you will see one of several outcomes.

Leave sees James accept Mary’s death, forgive himself and leave with Laura. Bitter-sweet.

Maria sees James cling to the fantasy, dooming himself to repeat the cycle.

In Water is tragic, bleak and fitting, with James unable to live with guilt.

Rebirth sees James plan to resurrect Mary with cult magic. Absurd, desperate and sad.

Dog reveals that a shiba inu has been pulling the strings the whole time.

UFO gives you an alien abduction ending, because Konami loves you and hates you in equal measure.

What is crucial is that these endings are not based on simple binary choices. They reflect you. Did you check on Maria often? Did you linger at Mary’s picture? Did you heal quickly?

The game watched.

And it judged.

## Born from a Wish: Maria’s tragic solo

Later versions of Silent Hill 2 included Born from a Wish, a short prequel chapter starring Maria.

It is only an hour or two, but it is loaded with nuance. Maria wanders the town, unsure of her identity, caught between existence and illusion.

She meets Ernest Baldwin, a reclusive figure in a decaying mansion. Their dialogue is cryptic and sorrowful. Ernest speaks of ghosts and memory. Maria, who may not even be alive in any conventional sense, finds only more questions.

It is a powerful coda.

Not essential, but enriching.

It asks: if Maria is fake, why does she feel so real?

## Puzzle design: the logic of nightmares

What separates Silent Hill 2 from its horror contemporaries is not just what it makes you feel, but what it makes you think.

The game’s puzzles are not arbitrary key-card hunts. They are symbolic, thematically resonant and just complex enough to demand engagement.

Take the infamous Coin Puzzle at the Wood Side Apartments. You are asked to place coins representing a snake, an old man and a prisoner into the correct slots based on a cryptic poem. It is a microcosm of Silent Hill 2’s design ethos. Every object, every riddle, is steeped in allegory.

This is not about unlocking doors.

It is about unlocking truths.

The difficulty settings for puzzles, ranging from Easy to the notorious Riddle Level: Hard, change not just the challenge but the nature of the solutions. Hard puzzles force players to interpret dense poetry and philosophical riddles.

It is a bold design choice that expects players to meet the game on its own terms.

## Development history: Team Silent’s uncompromising vision

Silent Hill 2 was developed by Konami’s internal group known as Team Silent, a collective of talented misfits who, following the success of the original Silent Hill, were given unprecedented freedom to create something more ambitious and artistic.

What they delivered in 2001 was nothing short of visionary.

The team deliberately set Silent Hill 2 apart from its predecessor. Gone were the overt occult themes and shared protagonists. Instead, they chose to reboot the franchise as an anthology of sorts, with different stories, different characters and the fog-shrouded town tying it all together.

Masashi Tsuboyama directed, Masahiro Ito returned as art director and monster designer, and Akira Yamaoka handled both music and sound. This tight-knit group worked with a unique creative synergy, drawing influence from David Lynch, Fyodor Dostoevsky and Francis Bacon.

Their goal was clear: not to frighten the player with gore, but to unsettle them with psychology.

And crucially, Konami let them do it.

No focus groups.

No franchise-mandated constraints.

Just pure, auteur-driven horror.

## Cultural legacy: the game that launched a thousand screams

Silent Hill 2 did not just influence horror. It redefined it.

Its legacy can be felt in everything from Dead Space to The Last of Us, Alan Wake to Amnesia. It proved that games could be emotionally resonant, morally ambiguous and narratively complex.

In film, its influence is clear too. Silent Hill’s own movie adaptation borrows liberally from Silent Hill 2’s imagery and tone. While no adaptation has ever truly captured the game’s nuance, the attempt speaks volumes.

And perhaps most tellingly: Pyramid Head became an icon.

He has turned up in merchandise, fan art and crossovers, but fans of Silent Hill 2 know he does not truly belong anywhere else.

He is not just a cool design.

He is James’ monster.

He is our monster.

## The legacy of a masterpiece

Two decades on, Silent Hill 2 stands tall.

The remake may reimagine its visuals, but the original PS2 version remains peerless for mood, meaning and memory.

It is a game that does not just tell a story. It makes you feel it.

Every scream.

Every silence.

Every shameful revelation.

This is horror not as spectacle, but as introspection.

You do not play Silent Hill 2 for the combat. You play it to remember what games can be.

Art.

Therapy.

Confession.

Catharsis.

## Final verdict: a town built from fear, a game built from brilliance

So, what is Silent Hill 2, really?

It is not a horror game.

Not just.

It is a tragedy. A confession. A philosophical meditation on guilt, love, memory and madness.

It is a game that does not hand you the truth. It makes you dig it up.

It does not give you closure. It makes you live with consequences.

It does not tell you who you are. It lets you discover that in a foggy reflection stained with regret.

On PlayStation 2, where memory cards were sacred and save files had soul, Silent Hill 2 was not just a title.

It was an event.

An unforgettable pilgrimage into the darkest corners of the human heart.

Lying Figures are humanoid, wrapped in straitjacket-like flesh. They spray acid, perhaps bile, perhaps venom, representing James’ suppression and internalised guilt.

Mannequins are leg-for-torso monstrosities, motionless until provoked. Their overt sexuality and violent movement speak to repression and desire.

Bubble Head Nurses are twisted icons of care turned threat. Fetishised, faceless and dangerous, they become manifestations of Mary’s illness and James’ conflicted lust.

Abstract Daddy, a contorted bedlike creature, appears during Angela’s scenes and is horrifyingly symbolic of her trauma.

No enemy is just an enemy.

Each is tied to suffering.

This is not creature design. This is psychoanalysis with teeth.

Gameplay loop: slow, methodical and maddeningly tense

Silent Hill 2 does not give you jump scares every five minutes.

It gives you dread.

Lingering, festering dread.

Combat is functional at best. James can swing pipes, swing boards or fire pistols with aim so shaky you would swear he was half-cut. But that is the point. He is not a soldier. He is broken.

The game does not want you to win fights. It wants you to fear them.

Exploration is masterful. Keys are hidden in disturbing places. Puzzles range from literary riddles about sin to strange item combinations that feel like they belong in a nightmare.

And the map system, scratched with handwritten notes, locked doors and red marks, keeps you grounded in a town designed to disorient.

Symbolism in everything: nothing is incidental

It cannot be overstated. Silent Hill 2 is a game that means.

Everything in it, every visual, every line, every ambient sound, carries symbolic weight.

The fog is not just atmospheric masking. It represents James’ self-imposed blindness.

The town morphs depending on the psychological state of each character. For James, it is a purgatory of guilt. For Angela, it is a hell of fire and abuse.

The mirrors found throughout the game reflect more than James’ image. They invite players to reflect on themselves.

Maria’s constant deaths are not cheap shock tactics. They are James’ guilt replaying his wife’s final moments over and over in slightly altered forms.

Rooms loop endlessly. Doors slam shut behind you. Architecture shifts without warning.

Everything plays into the dream logic of trauma and denial.

Then there is the final act: the Lakeview Hotel. This location is not just the climax of the plot. It is the truth laid bare. The waterlogged walls, dilapidated decor and haunted videotape are all steeped in narrative catharsis.

Soundtrack and sound design: Akira Yamaoka’s masterwork

If there is a Mount Rushmore of video game composers, Akira Yamaoka deserves a spot for Silent Hill 2 alone.

His soundtrack is a symphony of sorrow. From the melancholy chords of Theme of Laura to the ambient horror of the hospital basement, the music is the mood.

Equally impressive is the sound design. Floorboards creak. Radios hum. Wind howls. Distant groans reverberate through rusted halls. Each step asks a question:

“What did I just hear?”

The answer is rarely kind.

The voice acting is offbeat and stilted, but here is the strange thing: it works. The delivery feels dreamlike and otherworldly. It adds to the atmosphere rather than detracting from it.

Technical craftsmanship on the PS2

On paper, Silent Hill 2 does not push polygons like Metal Gear Solid 2 or animate crowds like Final Fantasy X.

But in terms of atmosphere, it is peerless.

The PlayStation 2 version achieves its emotional power through subtlety and manipulation.

Dynamic lighting plays a critical role. The player’s torchlight casts real-time shadows that jitter and stretch in disorienting ways, heightening tension. The grain filter gives the visuals a filmic quality that has aged far better than the clean, plasticky look of many early 2000s games.

On the audio front, the PS2’s sound chip is pushed to eerie limits. Stereo panning creates the illusion of creatures lurking just out of sight. The feedback from your radio, which crackles louder as enemies approach, becomes a Pavlovian trigger for panic.

Even the UI is minimalist. Your health is indicated by colour shifts rather than numbers, forcing you to engage intuitively, not analytically.

This technical minimalism is not a limitation.

It is design brilliance.

The end: who are you, James?

Few games have endings this reactive.

Depending on how you play, not what you choose, but what you do, you will see one of several outcomes.

Leave sees James accept Mary’s death, forgive himself and leave with Laura. Bitter-sweet.

Maria sees James cling to the fantasy, dooming himself to repeat the cycle.

In Water is tragic, bleak and fitting, with James unable to live with guilt.

Rebirth sees James plan to resurrect Mary with cult magic. Absurd, desperate and sad.

Dog reveals that a shiba inu has been pulling the strings the whole time.

UFO gives you an alien abduction ending, because Konami loves you and hates you in equal measure.

What is crucial is that these endings are not based on simple binary choices. They reflect you. Did you check on Maria often? Did you linger at Mary’s picture? Did you heal quickly?

The game watched.

And it judged.

Born from a Wish: Maria’s tragic solo

Later versions of Silent Hill 2 included Born from a Wish, a short prequel chapter starring Maria.

It is only an hour or two, but it is loaded with nuance. Maria wanders the town, unsure of her identity, caught between existence and illusion.

She meets Ernest Baldwin, a reclusive figure in a decaying mansion. Their dialogue is cryptic and sorrowful. Ernest speaks of ghosts and memory. Maria, who may not even be alive in any conventional sense, finds only more questions.

It is a powerful coda.

Not essential, but enriching.

It asks: if Maria is fake, why does she feel so real?

Puzzle design: the logic of nightmares

What separates Silent Hill 2 from its horror contemporaries is not just what it makes you feel, but what it makes you think.

The game’s puzzles are not arbitrary key-card hunts. They are symbolic, thematically resonant and just complex enough to demand engagement.

Take the infamous Coin Puzzle at the Wood Side Apartments. You are asked to place coins representing a snake, an old man and a prisoner into the correct slots based on a cryptic poem. It is a microcosm of Silent Hill 2’s design ethos. Every object, every riddle, is steeped in allegory.

This is not about unlocking doors.

It is about unlocking truths.

The difficulty settings for puzzles, ranging from Easy to the notorious Riddle Level: Hard, change not just the challenge but the nature of the solutions. Hard puzzles force players to interpret dense poetry and philosophical riddles.

It is a bold design choice that expects players to meet the game on its own terms.

Development history: Team Silent’s uncompromising vision

Silent Hill 2 was developed by Konami’s internal group known as Team Silent, a collective of talented misfits who, following the success of the original Silent Hill, were given unprecedented freedom to create something more ambitious and artistic.

What they delivered in 2001 was nothing short of visionary.

The team deliberately set Silent Hill 2 apart from its predecessor. Gone were the overt occult themes and shared protagonists. Instead, they chose to reboot the franchise as an anthology of sorts, with different stories, different characters and the fog-shrouded town tying it all together.

Masashi Tsuboyama directed, Masahiro Ito returned as art director and monster designer, and Akira Yamaoka handled both music and sound. This tight-knit group worked with a unique creative synergy, drawing influence from David Lynch, Fyodor Dostoevsky and Francis Bacon.

Their goal was clear: not to frighten the player with gore, but to unsettle them with psychology.

And crucially, Konami let them do it.

No focus groups.

No franchise-mandated constraints.

Just pure, auteur-driven horror.

Cultural legacy: the game that launched a thousand screams

Silent Hill 2 did not just influence horror. It redefined it.

Its legacy can be felt in everything from Dead Space to The Last of Us, Alan Wake to Amnesia. It proved that games could be emotionally resonant, morally ambiguous and narratively complex.

In film, its influence is clear too. Silent Hill’s own movie adaptation borrows liberally from Silent Hill 2’s imagery and tone. While no adaptation has ever truly captured the game’s nuance, the attempt speaks volumes.

And perhaps most tellingly: Pyramid Head became an icon.

He has turned up in merchandise, fan art and crossovers, but fans of Silent Hill 2 know he does not truly belong anywhere else.

He is not just a cool design.

He is James’ monster.

He is our monster.

The legacy of a masterpiece

Two decades on, Silent Hill 2 stands tall.

The remake may reimagine its visuals, but the original PS2 version remains peerless for mood, meaning and memory.

It is a game that does not just tell a story. It makes you feel it.

Every scream.

Every silence.

Every shameful revelation.

This is horror not as spectacle, but as introspection.

You do not play Silent Hill 2 for the combat. You play it to remember what games can be.

Art.

Therapy.

Confession.

Catharsis.

Final verdict: a town built from fear, a game built from brilliance

So, what is Silent Hill 2, really?

It is not a horror game.

Not just.

It is a tragedy. A confession. A philosophical meditation on guilt, love, memory and madness.

It is a game that does not hand you the truth. It makes you dig it up.

It does not give you closure. It makes you live with consequences.

It does not tell you who you are. It lets you discover that in a foggy reflection stained with regret.

On PlayStation 2, where memory cards were sacred and save files had soul, Silent Hill 2 was not just a title.

It was an event.

An unforgettable pilgrimage into the darkest corners of the human heart.

The Punisher – Brutal, Unforgiving, and Uniquely Retro
A PS2 review of The Punisher, Volition and THQ’s brutal Marvel shooter that turns Frank Castle’s war on crime into one of the grittiest licensed games of the era.
Brutal, dated, but still seriously satisfying
A rough but memorable Marvel shooter that captures Frank Castle’s violence, grit and grim simplicity better than most licensed games of its era.
Players who enjoy PS2-era shooters, Marvel anti-heroes, arcade violence and gritty licensed games.
The interrogation system, relentless tone and satisfying weapon-heavy combat.
Clunky aiming, plain boss fights and some mechanics that feel very early-2000s.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Justice with a skull on its chest

Some superhero games want you to feel powerful.

*The Punisher* wants you to feel dangerous.

Released on PlayStation 2 in 2005, Volition’s take on Marvel’s most uncompromising anti-hero is not elegant, subtle or particularly interested in restraint. This is a game built around gunfire, interrogations, criminal hideouts and Frank Castle’s total refusal to handle anything gently.

It is grim.

It is violent.

It is very, very PS2.

And that is exactly why it still has a strange kind of charm.

Where many licensed games from the era felt like rushed tie-ins or half-hearted adaptations, *The Punisher* understands the assignment. Frank Castle is not a colourful hero. He is not here to save the day with a speech. He is a walking vendetta in body armour, and the game builds almost every system around that idea.

This is not the most polished third-person shooter of its generation.

But it has identity.

And sometimes, identity matters more than polish.

## Frank Castle enters the room

The setup is simple, which is exactly how it should be.

You play as Frank Castle, better known as The Punisher, tearing through New York’s criminal underworld one violent encounter at a time. The story moves through mobsters, mercenaries, corrupt operations and familiar Marvel faces, giving the campaign enough comic-book flavour without burying the player in lore.

The plot is not the real draw here.

The draw is the fantasy.

Walking into a room as The Punisher should feel different from walking in as Spider-Man, Wolverine or Batman. There is no acrobatic grace. No gadgets doing the heavy lifting. No heroic restraint.

Frank solves problems with bullets, fists and fear.

The game understands that.

You are not really encouraged to play cleanly. You are encouraged to dominate the space, use the environment, break enemy morale and push through each encounter like a man with no interest in negotiation.

It is crude, but effective.

## Combat that does not overcomplicate itself

At its core, *The Punisher* is a third-person shooter with a simple loop.

Enter area.
Clear enemies.
Grab weapons.
Interrogate survivors.
Move deeper into the mess.

The shooting is straightforward and punchy. You get pistols, shotguns, assault rifles, SMGs and heavier weapons, with plenty of ammo scattered throughout the levels. There is a certain arcade feel to the combat. Frank can soak up punishment, dish out more, and keep moving like a one-man war machine.

By modern standards, the aiming is clunky.

There is no escaping that.

Movement can feel stiff. Locking onto enemies is not always as clean as you want it to be, and the camera occasionally reminds you that this is very much a mid-2000s action game.

But once you settle into its rhythm, the combat works.

Not because it is technically amazing, but because it suits the character. Frank does not need balletic gunplay. He needs blunt force. And *The Punisher* delivers blunt force in abundance.

The weapons have weight. Enemies drop quickly. Rooms explode into chaos. It all feels slightly ugly, but in a way that fits the tone.

## The interrogation system: grim, memorable and very of its time

The interrogation system is the mechanic everyone remembers.

Frank can grab enemies and force information out of them using threats, violence and environmental hazards. Some interrogations are standard intimidation sequences. Others take place near special hotspots, where the game pushes its already brutal tone into something more extreme.

It is one of the most distinctive parts of the game.

It is also the part that makes it feel most frozen in its era.

The violence is deliberately over-the-top, and the game famously uses a black-and-white filter during certain moments to soften the most graphic executions. That censorship almost makes the game stranger. Instead of making it feel cleaner, it gives those moments an odd exploitation-movie quality.

Like the game knows it has gone too far, but cannot quite stop itself.

Mechanically, interrogations give you information, secrets and momentum. Tonally, they remind you that Frank Castle is not a traditional hero. He is terrifying to the people he hunts.

Whether that lands for you will depend on your tolerance for the character and the era.

But as a piece of licensed game design, it is hard to deny how memorable it is.

## Rage mode and the joy of going too far

Frank’s rage mode is another standout.

When activated, the game pushes the violence into full comic-book absurdity. Frank becomes even more lethal, closing distance quickly and cutting through enemies with brutal close-range attacks. It is ridiculous, exaggerated and deeply satisfying when used at the right moment.

The best thing about rage mode is that it breaks up the shooting.

Instead of every encounter becoming the same stop-and-pop routine, rage gives you a pressure valve. When a fight starts slipping away or enemies begin to swarm, you can turn Frank into a walking nightmare and reassert control.

It is not subtle.

But nothing about this game is subtle.

That is part of the fun.

## Level design built for mayhem

The levels are mostly linear, but they usually give you enough room to cause trouble.

Warehouses, docks, mob hideouts and criminal compounds all serve as violent playgrounds for Frank’s mission. They are not especially complex spaces, but they are functional and varied enough to keep the campaign moving.

The game is at its best when a room has options.

A few enemies with guns.
A weapon rack.
A corner to use for cover.
An interrogation point.
A side path hiding a secret.

That is when *The Punisher* feels like more than a basic shooter. It becomes a grim little combat puzzle where the question is not “how do I survive?” but “how efficiently can I dismantle this room?”

The boss fights are weaker.

They are not terrible, but they rarely match the creativity of the core encounters. For a game this violent and personality-driven, you might expect more memorable set-piece battles. Instead, most bosses feel like functional obstacles rather than highlights.

They get the job done.

Not much more.

## Marvel cameos without losing the tone

One of the nicest surprises is how well the game uses Marvel characters.

You get appearances from familiar faces like Black Widow and Daredevil, and these cameos help connect Frank’s violent little corner of the world to the wider Marvel universe. The game does not turn into a superhero crossover circus, which is important.

It still feels like Frank’s story.

The cameos are seasoning, not the main course.

That is the right choice.

The voice acting is also better than you might expect from a licensed action game of this period. Frank sounds appropriately gruff and deadpan, while the supporting cast gives the game enough comic-book texture to stop it feeling completely one-note.

The dialogue can be dated, sure.

But it fits the game’s grimy tone.

## Presentation: mud, muzzle flashes and comic-book grit

Visually, *The Punisher* has aged in that particular PS2 way where some parts look rough and some parts still work because the art direction carries them.

The textures are muddy.
Character models are chunky.
Animations are stiff in places.

But the overall mood holds up.

This is a dark, dirty-looking game. It does not need to be beautiful. It needs to feel harsh, and it does. The colour palette, lighting and environments all lean into the idea of a criminal underworld being torn apart one hideout at a time.

The audio is stronger.

Weapons sound punchy. Explosions have impact. Enemies bark threats and panic under pressure. The music stays in the background, letting the sound of gunfire and chaos do most of the work.

There are also small details that help sell Frank as a veteran killer. The way he handles weapons, reloads, moves between targets and carries himself all contribute to the fantasy.

It is not a technically stunning game.

But it knows what it wants to feel like.

## Comparisons to Max Payne and Dead to Rights

It is hard not to compare *The Punisher* to other third-person action games of its time.

*Max Payne* had slow-motion gunplay and noir style.
*Dead to Rights* had disarms, takedowns and gritty crime drama.
*The Punisher* sits somewhere nearby, but with a nastier edge.

It does not have the mechanical slickness of *Max Payne*. It does not have the same cinematic flair. But it compensates with brutality, environmental violence and a stronger sense of character fantasy.

You are not just playing a shooter.

You are playing *The Punisher*.

That distinction matters.

The mechanics may be less refined than its peers, but the personality is stronger than many games that control better.

## Where the cracks show

The biggest problem with *The Punisher* is that its simplicity can become repetition.

There are only so many rooms full of enemies you can clear before the rhythm becomes predictable. The interrogation system helps, but even that can start to feel familiar after a while.

The aiming and movement also hold it back.

Modern players going back to this now may need time to adjust. It does not have the snap or fluidity people expect from newer third-person shooters. There is a heaviness to everything, and not always in an intentional way.

The bosses, as mentioned, are underwhelming.

And while the tone is faithful to the character, it can also be exhausting. This is a game that lives in one emotional register for long stretches: angry, violent and grim.

If you are not on board with Frank Castle as a character, the game will not win you over.

But if you are?

It delivers the fantasy better than expected.

## Why it still matters

*The Punisher* is worth revisiting because it represents a type of licensed game we do not get much anymore.

It is not trying to be a giant open-world service platform.
It is not trying to please every kind of Marvel fan.
It is not sanding down the character into something safer.

It picks a lane.

Then it drives through it with a skull painted on the bonnet.

That confidence gives the game staying power. It may be clunky. It may be dated. It may be too much at times. But it is rarely confused about what it wants to be.

For a mid-2000s Marvel game, that is impressive.

Many superhero games from this era survive mostly through nostalgia. *The Punisher* survives because it has a specific mood, a memorable mechanic and a strong grasp of its lead character.

That is enough to make it stand out.

## Final verdict

*The Punisher* on PS2 is not a masterpiece.

It is too stiff, too repetitive and too uneven to sit with the very best action games of its generation.

But it is memorable.

It captures Frank Castle’s brutality with surprising confidence, wraps it in a grim third-person shooter structure, and adds just enough mechanical identity through interrogations, rage mode and environmental violence to make it feel distinct.

It is a game from an era when licensed titles could be messy, aggressive and strange.

And honestly, that is part of its appeal.

If you want polish, there are better PS2 shooters.

If you want a brutal Marvel revenge fantasy with a black-and-white censorship filter, grimy levels and Frank Castle turning criminal hideouts into disaster zones, this still delivers.

It is not pretty.

It is not gentle.

It is very much The Punisher.

X-Men: Next Dimension – Mutant Mayhem With a Side of Clunk
A retro PS2 review of X-Men: Next Dimension, a clunky but charming comic-book fighter filled with fan service and early-2000s chaos.
A clunky but charming PS2-era fighter that still has value for X-Men fans.
PS2-era Marvel fans and players who enjoy messy licensed games.
Roster, fan service and early-2000s comic-book energy.
Clunky combat, rough visuals and uneven pacing.

## “They don’t make ’em like this anymore… and maybe that’s not a bad thing.”

Before superhero games became massive cinematic events, there was a strange little window where licensed comic-book games could be messy, ambitious and oddly charming.

*X-Men: Next Dimension* sits right in that era.

A 3D fighter packed with mutants, dramatic story beats, alternate costumes and enough early-2000s clunk to make you question whether nostalgia is doing some heavy lifting.

## Enter the Danger Room

At its best, *Next Dimension* is a celebration of the X-Men universe.

The roster is genuinely impressive for the time, with a chunky selection of heroes and villains, each bringing their own powers, specials and personality to the fight.

There is also a proper attempt at story, which is more than you can say for a lot of fighting games from this period. It leans into Marvel drama, comic-book stakes and big mutant chaos in a way that gives the game more identity than expected.

It is rough, yes.

But it is not lazy.

## Krakoa cracks begin to show

The problems start when the fighting actually begins.

The combat is stiff, the movement lacks flow and combos never quite feel as smooth as they should. You can see the idea behind the game, but the execution often feels like it is fighting against itself.

The camera does not always help either.

For a game trying to sell dramatic 3D arenas and environmental transitions, it can be surprisingly easy to lose your sense of position mid-fight.

## The good, the bad and the fugly

Visually, *X-Men: Next Dimension* is a product of its time.

Some stages have personality. Some special attacks still have a bit of comic-book flair. But character models and animations can look awkward, especially when compared with stronger fighters from the same era.

Still, there is charm under the grime.

The game clearly wants to be more than a throwaway licensed fighter. It wants to give X-Men fans a full roster, story mode, flashy powers and dramatic mutant battles.

That ambition matters.

## Time travel troubles

Looking back now, *X-Men: Next Dimension* feels like a relic from a very specific gaming era.

It is the kind of game that probably would not get made in the same way today. It is too strange, too specific and too rough around the edges.

But that is also why it is interesting.

It captures a time when licensed games could be flawed, experimental and weirdly full of personality.

## Verdict

*X-Men: Next Dimension* is not a hidden masterpiece.

It is clunky, uneven and visually rough.

But it is also packed with fan service, ambition and that particular PS2-era charm that makes flawed games worth revisiting.

You might not love every fight, but if you grew up with X-Men, Marvel games or early-2000s licensed chaos, there is still something here worth remembering.
